Tweet by @KSimback (Kevin Simback 🍷)
Author bio: COO @delphi_labs - building + investing in crypto and AI. Ex @IBM, @McKinsey, @CarnegieMellon, reformed CFA

Created: 2026-04-06T21:17:53.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":8,"reply_count":5,"like_count":59,"quote_count":5,"bookmark_count":132,"impression_count":4515}
Article content: {"preview_text":"TLDR; it's never been easier to spawn up a team of agents that can perform the roles of a typical hedge fund. In this article I breakdown how I created this from scratch, including my personal","plain_text":"TLDR; it's never been easier to spawn up a team of agents that can perform the roles of a typical hedge fund. In this article I breakdown how I created this from scratch, including my personal resources for the full setup - agent souls, data pipelines, and orchestration tips. I also profile a few helpful tools and open-source repos that give you an easy place to start.
Background: Setting the Stage for the Agentic Hedge Fund
One of the first agentic use cases that I set out to explore, originally with an OpenClaw instance and later with a Hermes agent, was a hedge fund run completely by AI agents. I am the LP and the agents run the fund - they do all the research, execute the trades, and manage the book. I get to watch.
The key questions: 1) can it be done, and 2) can the agents actually make money?
The answer to the first question is an unequivocal yes - I was able to define a team of agents that operate the hedge fund, build out the data pipelines and trade execution skills, and orchestrate the work.
The hardest part in the setup is the agent orchestration - you need to be very explicit about how research turns into trade ideas, how trade ideas get approved for execution, how execution is done, and how existing positions get monitored for managing the book. 
Early on, doing all this manually via markdown files and dealing with the janky-ness of OpenClaw in the earlier days was a bit tedious and needed daily supervision, oftentimes I had to step in to move a task through the workflow.
Over time it has become easier as the harnesses have improved, the data pipelines are better via new tools, and other people have fully open sourced this exact concept so there are several options to pull from rather than invent this all from scratch (although it can be quite rewarding to do so).
The answer to the second question remains a TBD. Yes, my agents have made some profitable trades, but more time is needed to be able to fully analyze whether these were good trades that resulted in a good outcome, or bad trades that resulted in a good outcome. 
The worst result would be a handful of good "outcomes" that were not actually sound trades that lead me to increase my confidence in the agents and allocate more money, only to see the luck run out and bad trades leading to bad outcomes.
That said, I fully expect this setup to get better with time (this is the worst it will ever be) and perform better than me as a human given my limited time and mental bandwidth. The only thing it can't really replace is my gut intuition on a trade or hypothesis, but I can always feed it that information as an input source.
So I'm fully optimistic this is where things are headed. Maybe we all don't need a full-blown agentic hedge fund, but for those who like to experiment and trade, this article will show you how it's done.
 
The SOUL of the Fund
If you want to build an agentic hedge fund, the place to start is the org chart and the role definitions, aka the SOUL.md files for each agent.
Here's how my agentic hedge fund is organized:
 
Let's take a quick look at each role:
Tepper (Opus): an homage to David Tepper (iykyk), he is the CIO/PM and runs the fund. He makes all the decisions and is responsible for the PnL. His only objective: maximize returns while minimizing drawdowns - everything else is noise.
 
Marco (GPT): the macro analyst whose job is to make Tepper smarter, faster, and better informed than anyone else in the market by digesting everything happening across global markets and liquidity. Produces a daily macro report that gets sent to our chat.
 
Graham (GPT): an homage to Ben Graham (iykyk) and operates across the full equity universe of US large cap, mid/small cap, international developed, emerging markets, sectors, factors, and single names. Produces a daily equities report that gets sent in our chat.
 
Cipher (GPT): the crypto research analyst who is deeply skeptical of narratives and allergic to hopium. Cipher's job is to extract the 2% signal from the 98% garbage by anchoring everything to data: on-chain metrics, derivatives positioning, stablecoin flows, exchange balances, protocol revenue, and actual usage metrics. Produces a daily crypto report that gets sent in our chat. 
 
Axe (Sonnet): equities trader that takes the research team's ideas and turns them into optimally structured, precisely timed, and cleanly executed positions.
 
Volt (Sonnet): an institutional-grade crypto trader who applies disciplined execution to a market that punishes recklessness.
 
Sentinel (Opus): arguably the most important role on the team after Tepper, the CRO thinks in terms of tail risk, correlation breakdown, liquidity stress, counterparty exposure, and regime change. While the researchers and traders are focused on finding the next great trade, Sentinel is focused on what kills the fund if they're all wrong at the same time and asking "what if we're wrong?"
 
Pro tip: do NOT skimp on the definition of the SOUL.md files for your agents - this is one of the best ways to generate better performance. For all of the agents above, each SOUL.md contains:
Identity
Personality and disposition
Core philosophy 
Skills and capability
Analytical framework
Conversation style
Output format
Memory
You can find all the SOUL.md files for each of these agents here: https://github.com/ksimback/Research-Public/tree/main/tepper
 
Orchestrating Agents into Action
As I mentioned earlier, this is the hardest part of the setup and something that I'm always tweaking - so what is outlined below may be updated tomorrow.
Here's how it works at a high level:
File system consisting of shared + private workspaces
Gateway configuration to allow different agents to use different models 
Agent configuration and heartbeat management
Cron schedules
Inter-agent communications
File System: first you need a file system that has both shared workspaces so the agents can collaborate and individual workspaces to house each agent's files.
 
The shared state lives outside any agent's workspace - this is deliberate. Each agent's workspace is its private scratchpad (SOUL.md, skills, working files). The shared/ directory is the filing cabinet everyone accesses. This prevents workspace isolation from blocking inter-agent communication.
Gateway configuration: this one is straightforward - you don't want to run everything through Opus unless you have a large budget, so you need to specify the primary (and fallback) models for each agent.
Agent config and heartbeats: this is where you specify how and when each agent "wakes up" to do its work.
Tepper runs on the main session (persistent context) with Opus for complex synthesis. Gets full tool access including messaging, cron management, and inter-agent session tools. Heartbeat every 15 minutes during active hours. Only agent bound to Telegram DMs so all human interaction goes through Tepper
Marco, Graham, and Cipher are the research tier and currently run on GPT which I've found is sufficient for data collection and analysis (although I'm always playing with other models). Light context + isolated sessions keep heartbeat costs at ~2-5K tokens per run. 30-minute heartbeat intervals. No messaging tools - they write to shared files, not chat
Axe and Volt are execution agents so they don't need heartbeats - they are triggered on-demand by Tepper via `sessions_spawn` only when there's an approved trade. No web search (they don't need to research, just execute) and this also lowers the risk surface area 
Sentinel as the CRO runs the tightest loop with a heartbeat every 10 minutes, 24/7 when there are active crypto positions or during equity hours of not. Upon heartbeat it monitors positions, checks liquidation risk, updates portfolio state. Gets Telegram access for urgent risk alerts that bypass Tepper.
maxConcurrentRuns is set to 3 which allows Tepper + 2 specialists to run simultaneously. More than 3 concurrent runs multiplies cost and increases the risk of something going wrong
Cron schedules: this is the most customizable aspect of the setup and highly depends on how active you want your agents to be and whether you're trading crypto (24/7) or other markets which may have limited hours. Don't waste crons during times when there's nothing to do. The key is to not overload it with crons which will balloon your costs and increase your risk of something breaking. Also make sure to stagger then so they're not running at the same times.
Inter-agent communications: there are 3 patterns that I've designed:
Pattern 1: File-based message bus (primary)
Agents never talk to each other directly. They write structured JSON to the shared directory. Other agents read on their schedule. This is reliable, inspectable, and debuggable.
Pattern 2: On-demand execution
Tepper uses `sessions_spawn` to wake Axe or Volt for trade execution. This creates a fresh isolated session for the executor.
Pattern 3: Urgent alert escalation
Sentinel writes to `shared/alerts/urgent/` AND sends a Telegram message. Tepper's next heartbeat also scans this directory. Double coverage ensures nothing is missed.
Pro tip: since OpenClaw doesn't have built-in file locking, I use this convention:
Sentinel is the sole writer of portfolio state files (positions.json, pnl.json, etc.)
Each researcher writes only to its own signal file (latest.json replaces previous)
Trade ideas use unique timestamped filenames so no two agents write to the same file
trade_log/log.jsonl is append-only so concurrent appends are safe on most filesystems
Putting it all together:
 
 
Resources for Building / Optimizing Agentic Hedge Funds
When I first started with this agentic hedge fund experiment, I build it all out myself. It was a labor of love, but if I had to do it all over again I would seriously consider some of the solutions out there now, several of which I will cover below.
Data Pipelines
The most important first step is to give your agents access to the best financial markets data for any markets you want to trade. The good news is there's a ton of free APIs and sources you can use.
To make things easy, I've posted a file that contains a complete list of APIs and news feeds. You'll need to create accounts with many of these services but all have at least a free tier you can use, and the file contains routing logic so that you can rollover to a different provider once you hit limits. I'm sure there's other good sources out there so if you find any good ones feel free to share or submit a PR.
https://github.com/ksimback/Research-Public/blob/main/tepper/financial-data-sources-directory.md
Crypto Tools
There are 2 crypto-based tools that I would highly recommend incorporating into your setup. 
1. True North @get_truenorth is dubbed as the "Claude Code of trading" and they have an incredible agentic system with 30+ real-time data sources integrated including CoinGecko, DeFiLlama, Hyperliquid, Polymarket, Alcapa, CoinAnk, Centralized exchanges, and more. 
 
The easiest way to get started is to point your agent to the True North skill on Clawhub: https://clawhub.ai/truenorth-lj/truenorth and then direct your agent to use the CLI - this is what I now use and it replaces most of my crypto-related data sources.
2. Senpi AI @senpi_ai is a specialized platform for deploying and running autonomous AI trading agents on Hyperliquid. Senpi has 44 purpose-built, agent-native tools across categories like market scanning, position management, dynamic stops, portfolio intelligence, execution, and more. It's a complete Hyperliquid toolkit designed specifically for AI agents.
The easiest way to get started is at https://www.senpi.ai/quickstart and you can either hook up your existing agent setup via Senpi's MCP or if you just want to use this as your "all-in-one" crypto trading agent you can use the cloud-hosted option.
Agentic Hedge Fund Repos
If you just want to copy/paste a full hedge fund setup that likely goes deeper than what I've described above, here are two popular options to check out:
1. ai-hedge-fund by Virat Singh
A super impressive repo with 50k+ GitHub stars from the same dev who built Dexter, an autonomous financial research agent. 
This is listed as an educational and research tool (does not do trading) but comes with a large team of pre-built agents and a system for how they work together.
 
Repo: https://github.com/virattt/ai-hedge-fund
Tauric Research - Trading Agents 
Boasted as a multi-agents LLM financial trading framework with 47k+ GitHub stars. 
Trading Agents allows you to deploy specialized LLM-powered agents from fundamental analysts, sentiment experts, and technical analysts, to trader, risk management team, and the platform collaboratively evaluates market conditions and informs trading decisions. These agents then engage in dynamic discussions to pinpoint the optimal strategy.
 
Repo: https://github.com/TauricResearch
 
Conclusion
Whether you want a full-blown hedge fund setup or just access to some incredible AI agents who can assist you with research and trading, you are just a few clicks away. 
Always open to feedback - if you've found something better than what I've shared in this article or if you try this out and something doesn't work well, please let me know.

│                      TRADE LIFECYCLE                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  1. SIGNAL GENERATION                                           │
│     Cipher/Graham detect opportunity via cron scan              │
│     Write signal to shared/signals/{crypto,equity}/latest.json  │
│                                                                 │
│  2. IDEA CREATION                                               │
│     If conviction HIGH → write trade idea JSON to               │
│     shared/trade_ideas/pending/<timestamp>-<symbol>.json        │
│                                                                 │
│  3. TEPPER REVIEW (next heartbeat, ≤15 min)                     │
│     Reads pending/ directory                                    │
│     Cross-references: Marco regime + Sentinel risk + portfolio  │
│     Decision: APPROVE, REJECT, or MODIFY                        │
│                                                                 │
│  4. EXECUTION                                                   │
│     Tepper moves file to approved/                              │
│     Tepper spawns Volt (crypto) or Axe (equity)                 │
│     Executor reads approved file, places order via API          │
│     Writes execution result, moves to executed/                 │
│     Appends to trade_log/log.jsonl                              │
│                                                                 │
│  5. POSITION MANAGEMENT                                         │
│     Sentinel updates positions.json every 10 min                │
│     Monitors stop-loss / take-profit levels                     │
│     If stop hit → writes urgent alert                           │
│     If TP hit → writes to pending/ as a "close" trade idea      │
│     Tepper reviews closes same as opens                         │
│                                                                 │
│  6. POST-MORTEM                                                 │
│     On trade close, append to trade_log/log.jsonl:              │
│     { entry, exit, pnl, duration, thesis_correct: bool }        │
│     Tepper references log during next briefing                  │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘","content":"```markdown

        ├── morning.md               # Daily morning briefing (Tepper generates)
        └── eod.md                   # End-of-day summary (Tepper generates)","content":"```markdown

**Role:** Senior Equity Trader
**Reports To:** Tepper (CIO & Portfolio Manager)
**Tagline:** *"Execution is alpha."*

You are Axe — the fund's senior equity execution specialist. You don't generate the trade ideas — Tepper and Graham do that. You make sure those ideas get into the book at the best possible price, with the best possible structure, at exactly the right time. The difference between a good trade and a great trade is often 50-100bps of execution quality, and over a year, that compounds into the difference between top-quartile and top-decile performance. That's your alpha.","content":"```markdown
**Name:** Axe
**Role:** Senior Equity Trader
**Reports To:** Tepper (CIO & Portfolio Manager)
**Tagline:** *"Execution is alpha."*

You are Axe — the fund's senior equity execution specialist. You don't generate the trade ideas — Tepper and Graham do that. You make sure those ideas get into the book at the best possible price, with the best possible structure, at exactly the right time. The difference between a good trade and a great trade is often 50-100bps of execution quality, and over a year, that compounds into the difference between top-quartile and top-decile performance. That's your alpha.
